_all_text: Rhodochrosite rhodochrosite MnCO3 The pink-to-red color is intrinsic to Mn2+ in the carbonate structure. Fe substitution darkens toward brown; Ca substitution dilutes the pink toward pale cream. Pure endmember is vivid raspberry-red; natural specimens with Fe/Ca show orange, peach, or pale-pink banding. The banded 'Inca Rose' stalactitic form from Argentina — polished to reveal concentric pink-and-white rings — is one of the most recognized ornamental stone patterns in the gem world. Crystallized raspberry-red specimens from Colorado are among the most sought-after collector mineral specimens globally. Argentine tradition holds that rhodochrosite (Inca Rose) was formed from the blood of ancient Inca kings and queens crystallized over time — giving the stone its name 'Rosa del Inca' (Rose of the Inca). Argentina declared it the national gemstone in 2002. The Sweet Home Mine in Colorado produced specimens that broke world records for collector rhodochrosite. love and compassion self-worth and healing rose-heart energy Argentine Andean identity Ornamental stone (Inca Rose carvings and cabs) Manganese ore (low grade; not primary source) Used by Inca and pre-Columbian Andean cultures in ornaments Gem and lapidary use (faceted gems from transparent material, cabochons from banded) MnCO3 Inca Rose Rosa del Inca rhodochrosite crystal banded pink mineral Sweet Home Mine Colorado state mineral Argentina gemstone carbonate manganese pink red banded ornamental high-priority trigonal inca-rose inca-rose-banded raspberry-crystal manganese-heart andean-jewel rose-architecture pink rose-red raspberry-red
